The **Porsche 718** is a series of one- or two-seat [sports-racing](/wiki/Sports_car_racing "Sports car racing") cars built by [Porsche](/wiki/Porsche "Porsche") from 1957 to 1962. An [open-wheel](/wiki/Open-wheel_car "Open-wheel car") single-seat model was developed for Formula racing.

## Details

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Details")]

The 718 was a development of the successful [Porsche 550A](/wiki/Porsche_550 "Porsche 550") with improvements made to the body work and suspension. The car's full name is 718 RSK, where "RS" stands for *RennSport* (sports-racing) and the "K" reflects the shape of the car's revised torsion-bar suspension.[[3]](#cite_note-tt-718-3) It had a [mid-engined](/wiki/Mid-engine_design "Mid-engine design") layout and used the 142 horsepower (106 kW) 1.5-litre Type 547/3 quad-cam engine introduced in the 550A.

## Variations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Variations")]

### 718 RSK Mittellenker

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 718 RSK Mittellenker")]

In 1957 the [Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile](/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_Internationale_de_l%27Automobile "Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile") (FIA) changed their rules to allow cars with enveloping bodywork to compete in Formula races.[[4]](#cite_note-leffingwellbook-4): 27 That year Porsche entered three 550/1500RS Spyders in the [1957 German Grand Prix](/wiki/1957_German_Grand_Prix "1957 German Grand Prix") [Formula Two](/wiki/Formula_Two "Formula Two") (F2) event. Changes to the cars were minimal, being limited to removing the passenger seats and spare tires.[[5]](#cite_note-msm-nov2011-5)

For 1958 Porsche fielded a modified 718, called the RSK Mittellenker (centre-steer), for F2 events.[[6]](#cite_note-stuttcars-718-6) The bodywork for this car was only slightly different from the sportscar model, but the single seat was now in the centre of the cockpit, with the steering wheel, pedals, and shift lever relocated to accommodate the change and a fairing enclosing more of the cockpit opening.[[7]](#cite_note-longracingbook-7): 65 [Jean Behra](/wiki/Jean_Behra "Jean Behra") drove the car to a win at the F2 event at Reims that year. At the German Grand Prix at the [Nürburgring](/wiki/N%C3%BCrburgring "Nürburgring"), driver [Edgar Barth](/wiki/Edgar_Barth "Edgar Barth") placed sixth overall and second in his class. At the Berlin Grand Prix at AVUS the car won both its heat and the F2 class in the hands of driver [Masten Gregory](/wiki/Masten_Gregory "Masten Gregory").

### 718/2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 718/2")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Porsche_718_2-02_F1_%281%29.jpg/250px-Porsche_718_2-02_F1_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Porsche_718_2-02_F1_(1).jpg)

Porsche 718 2-02

In 1959 Porsche unveiled the prototype of a narrow, open-wheeled car called the Porsche 718/2 that married the 718's mechanicals with a more traditional single-seat Formula body.[[8]](#cite_note-porsche-718-2-8) The unpainted car was entered in the 1959 Monaco Grand Prix, where driver [Wolfgang von Trips](/wiki/Wolfgang_von_Trips "Wolfgang von Trips") qualified twelfth, but crashed on the second lap of the race. At Reims driver [Joakim Bonnier](/wiki/Joakim_Bonnier "Joakim Bonnier") finished third. For 1960 the production 718/2, starting with chassis number 718201, received revised bodywork, a 6-speed transaxle, and a wheelbase extended by 100 mm (3.9 in).[[5]](#cite_note-msm-nov2011-5)[[9]](#cite_note-whitelockbook-9): 278–281 A total of five cars were built.

In October 1958 the FIA announced another change to the regulations for Formula One (F1).[[10]](#cite_note-long911book-10): 12 Beginning with the 1961 season, engine capacity would be limited to the same 1.5 litres as Formula Two. This allowed Porsche to use their 718/2s almost unchanged in F1.

### RS 60

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: RS 60")]

For the 1960 season the [FIA](/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_Internationale_de_l%27Automobile "Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile") made changes to the regulation regarding the windscreen and cockpit size. Applying these changes, together with a larger (1.6-litre) Type 547/3 engine developing 160 horsepower (120 kW) and a new double wishbone rear suspension, resulted in the RS 60 model. The RS 60 brought Porsche victory at the 1960 [12 Hours of Sebring](/wiki/12_Hours_of_Sebring "12 Hours of Sebring") with a car driven by [Hans Herrmann](/wiki/Hans_Herrmann "Hans Herrmann") and [Olivier Gendebien](/wiki/Olivier_Gendebien "Olivier Gendebien"). 1960 also saw Porsche win the [Targa Florio](/wiki/1960_Targa_Florio "1960 Targa Florio") with Hans Herrmann being joined on the winner podium by [Jo Bonnier](/wiki/Joakim_Bonnier "Joakim Bonnier") and [Graham Hill](/wiki/Graham_Hill "Graham Hill"). The RS 60 also ensured that Porsche successfully defended their European Hill Climb Championship for the third year in a row.

### RS 61

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: RS 61")]

For 1961 the model name was changed to "RS 61" although it was almost identical to the RS 60. An RS 61 won the European Hill Climb Championship.[[11]](#cite_note-rs61-11)

### W-RS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: W-RS")]

The W-RS version was developed in 1961. Initially fitted with a 4-cylinder engine, the car was later fitted with Porsche's air-cooled [Type 771](/wiki/Porsche_flat-eight_engines#Type_771 "Porsche flat-eight engines") 2.0 L [flat-eight engine](/wiki/Flat-eight_engine "Flat-eight engine") which produced 240 horsepower (180 kW).[[12]](#cite_note-w-rs-12) A W-RS finished 8th at Le Mans in [1963](/wiki/1963_24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "1963 24 Hours of Le Mans"). The W-RS continued Porsche's success in the European Hill Climb Championship with Edgar Barth claiming the title in 1963. Porsche would go on to win a European Hill Climb Championship every year until 1982, a total of 42 titles.

The W-RS continued racing until 1964 when it was replaced by the [904](/wiki/Porsche_904 "Porsche 904").

### 718 GTR Coupé

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 718 GTR Coupé")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/1962-05-27_%281%29_Porsche_von_Bonnier-Gurney%2C_Ende_Zielgerade.jpg/250px-1962-05-27_%281%29_Porsche_von_Bonnier-Gurney%2C_Ende_Zielgerade.jpg)](/wiki/File:1962-05-27_(1)_Porsche_von_Bonnier-Gurney,_Ende_Zielgerade.jpg)

Gurney/Bonnier Porsche 718 GTR at the Nürburgring in 1962.

A Coupé version was developed from the RS 61. Initially fitted with a 4-cylinder engine, this car was also upgraded to an 8-cylinder F1 derived engine which produced 210 horsepower (160 kW).[[13]](#cite_note-gtr-coupe-13) The car was also fitted with disc brakes. A GTR Coupé enabled drivers Jo Bonnier and [Carlo Maria Abate](/wiki/Carlo_Maria_Abate "Carlo Maria Abate") to win the [Targa Florio in 1963](/wiki/1963_Targa_Florio "1963 Targa Florio") once more.

## Racing history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Racing history")]

### Endurance / sportscar racing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Endurance / sportscar racing")]

The 718 made its racing debut at the [1957 24 Hours of Le Mans](/wiki/1957_24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "1957 24 Hours of Le Mans") driven by [Umberto Maglioli](/wiki/Umberto_Maglioli "Umberto Maglioli") and [Edgar Barth](/wiki/Edgar_Barth "Edgar Barth"). The car failed to finish the race due to an accident.

In 1958, the RSK Spyder #29 (1.6-litre engine) finished third overall at [Le Mans](/wiki/1958_24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "1958 24 Hours of Le Mans") guided by [Jean Behra](/wiki/Jean_Behra "Jean Behra") and [Hans Herrmann](/wiki/Hans_Herrmann "Hans Herrmann"). Right after them, in fourth place overall and first in class came the RSK #31 (1.5-litre engine), piloted by Edgar Barth and Paul Frère. Jean Behra also drove one of the cars home finishing second at the [Targa Florio](/wiki/Targa_Florio "Targa Florio") (an FIA [World Sportscar Championship](/wiki/World_Sportscar_Championship "World Sportscar Championship") race from 1955–1973).

In 1959, the car, driven by Edgar Barth and [Wolfgang Seidel](/wiki/Wolfgang_Seidel "Wolfgang Seidel"), achieved overall victory at the [1959 Targa Florio](/wiki/1959_Targa_Florio "1959 Targa Florio") for its first time. A 718 also won the [European Hill Climb Championship](/wiki/European_Hill_Climb_Championship "European Hill Climb Championship") in both 1958 and 1959. A 718 RS60 won the Targa Florio again in 1960.

For 1961, Ferrari put the V6 Dino engine not only in the back of the F1 cars, but also into the rear-mid-engine [Ferrari SP](/wiki/Ferrari_SP "Ferrari SP"). With good handling and superior power from 2.4 litres, Ferrari won the Targa in 1962 and 1963. In that year, the Scuderia also moved the big V12 behind the driver, starting the [Ferrari P](/wiki/Ferrari_P "Ferrari P") series. The principle pioneered by Porsche since the 1930s finally was adopted by others.

In 1961 [Masten Gregory](/wiki/Masten_Gregory "Masten Gregory") and Bob Holbert piloted a 718/4 RS Spyder to a class win at [Le Mans](/wiki/1961_24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "1961 24 Hours of Le Mans"). In 1963, Porsche got another Targa Florio victory, with a 718 GTR Coupé, making it three wins at that event for a 718 car.

### Formula Two

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Formula Two")]

Porsche made their F2 debut with victories at [Reims](/wiki/Reims-Gueux "Reims-Gueux")[[14]](#cite_note-f2-rheims-14) and [AVUS](/wiki/AVUS "AVUS")[[15]](#cite_note-f2-avus-15) in 1958. In 1959 other RSK's were converted for single seater racing and at the XV B.A.R.C. '200' at Aintree in 1960 Porsche scored a 1–2–3 victory with [Stirling Moss](/wiki/Stirling_Moss "Stirling Moss"), Jo Bonnier and Graham Hill all in 718s.[[16]](#cite_note-f2-aintree-16) The feat was repeated again later in the year at the race at [Zeltweg](/wiki/Zeltweg_Airfield "Zeltweg Airfield"), Austria.[[17]](#cite_note-f2-flugplatz-17)

### Formula One

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche_718&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Formula One")]

[Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") switched to a 1.5-litre formula in [1961](/wiki/1961_Formula_One_season "1961 Formula One season") and Porsche entered three 718s for [Dan Gurney](/wiki/Dan_Gurney "Dan Gurney"), [Hans Herrmann](/wiki/Hans_Herrmann "Hans Herrmann") and [Jo Bonnier](/wiki/Joakim_Bonnier "Joakim Bonnier"). Gurney scored three 2nd places ([France](/wiki/1961_French_Grand_Prix "1961 French Grand Prix"), [Italy](/wiki/1961_Italian_Grand_Prix "1961 Italian Grand Prix") and [United States](/wiki/1961_United_States_Grand_Prix "1961 United States Grand Prix")) taking him to 4th place in the Drivers' Championship. For [1962](/wiki/1962_Formula_One_season "1962 Formula One season"), Porsche entered a new car, the [Porsche 804](/wiki/Porsche_804 "Porsche 804") in Formula One.

[Carel Godin de Beaufort](/wiki/Carel_Godin_de_Beaufort "Carel Godin de Beaufort"), a privateer, entered a 718 in F1 between [1961](/wiki/1961_Formula_One_season "1961 Formula One season") and [1964](/wiki/1964_Formula_One_season "1964 Formula One season"). He was killed driving his 718 during practice for the [1964 German Grand Prix](/wiki/1964_German_Grand_Prix "1964 German Grand Prix") at the [Nürburgring](/wiki/N%C3%BCrburgring "Nürburgring").[[18]](#cite_note-CGdB-races-18)
